全球冠状病毒大流行使我们更加意识到时间是主观的。对于一些喜欢在家工作的人来说，日子过得很快。对于那些急于旅行或探望所爱之人的人来说，时间已经慢得像爬行一样。
发明时钟是为了帮助我们跟踪时间的流逝--然而，在某些时刻，当我们盯着时钟看的时候，我们会意识到一秒钟可以感觉到多长时间。
这两个时段的长度相等，尽管人们通常说，第一个时段-黄色方块可见的时候-感觉比第二个时段长，第二个时段是两个黄色正方形之间的空白或未填满的空隙。
根据神经学家的说法，人体内没有一个器官或系统负责计时。事实上，心理学家已经确定了许多影响我们时间意识的因素，其中一些因素解释了我们今年对时间意识的增强。
在这个练习中，你被要求在事件发生后做出时间估计，或者心理学家所说的回溯性判断。
芬兰图尔库大学的哲学讲师瓦尔特里·阿尔斯蒂拉(Valtteri Arstila)解释说，你可以做出这个判断，因为你在工作记忆中有第一个要比较的形状。虽然这种方法可以比较非常短的持续时间，但对于较长的时间段则使用不同的过程。
回想一下，当你被要求呆在家里以防止新的冠状病毒传播的时候。那段时间是过得快还是过得慢？
例如，大多数纽约人被要求从3月份的某个时候开始呆在家里，许多人似乎注意到，尽管有重复的日子，4月份还是飞逝而过。
加州大学圣地亚哥分校(University Of California San Diego)哲学教授克雷格·卡伦德(Craig Callender)解释说，我们做出这一判断是基于我们从长期记忆中回忆起的一件事。
他说：“如果你把每个重要事件都看作是时钟的滴答声，4月份就没有那么多滴答声，所以感觉时间过得真的很快。”
您刚刚观看了一系列图像，这些图像显示的时间相同，尽管新奇的图像，在本例中是嵌套的正方形，通常被认为持续时间更长。
这就是所谓的怪异效应。阿尔斯蒂拉指出了两个现象，它们可以帮助我们理解我们是如何处理重复周期的。
首先，我们在记忆中已经有了重复物体的记录，因此我们不太注意，感觉它过得更快。其次，当新的物体出现时，我们的注意力集中在创造新的记忆上，使时间感觉好像慢了下来。
Arstila说，抑制重复的日子是我们可能记得滞留在家里的时间过得很快的原因之一。他说，同样的影响也解释了为什么经常过着例行公事的退休人员会说时间过得飞快。
许多研究关注了注意力和情绪是如何影响我们的时间感的。重新创建说明性示例存在挑战，但请尝试以下测试。
您刚刚执行了间隔时间估计。三种刺激显示的持续时间相同，尽管不断扩大的圆圈通常被认为持续的时间更长。
Arstila说：“吸引我们注意力或需要更多关注的东西感觉要长得多。”
“对时间的估计与人们的情绪最相关。如果你玩得很开心，时间过得飞快，如果你感到悲伤、孤独，那么时间就会流逝。“阿斯蒂拉说。
忙碌的一天通常过得很快，但对一些人来说，2020年的情况正好相反。例如，前线医护人员知道他们接触冠状病毒的风险很高，由此产生的焦虑会提高他们的注意力，减缓他们对一天过得有多快的感知。
然而，对于其他人来说，时间可以在快乐的时刻飞逝，比如视频追赶或与朋友共进晚餐。
你可能发现自己最近在思考你最后一次与朋友见面或去健身房是什么时候。我们对最近和很久以前的事件的回忆以一种奇怪的方式扭曲。
想一想最近的一次约会，估计它发生在多长时间之前，来测试一下自己。然后检查你的日历，看看它实际发生的时间。或者，在没有帮助的情况下，试着对以下事件进行约会。
我们倾向于依靠记忆而不是知识来确定发生在我们有生之年的事件的日期。但是，当我们的记忆扭曲了我们对时间的感知时，它也会影响我们对事件何时发生的感觉。
心理学家发现，当回忆起很久以前的事件时，认为它发生得比实际上更近是很常见的。但是，如果事件发生在过去三年内，我们往往认为它发生在更早的时间之前。
这种效应叫做伸缩。可以把它想象成通过望远镜向后或向前看，图像会根据方向发生扭曲。
鉴于今年卫生纸短缺，你还记得上一次买卫生纸是什么时候吗？记住，记忆会随着时间的推移而褪色，你的回忆可能会经历伸缩。
2020年不会很快被遗忘为大流行之年，但我们很可能会弄错一些事件发生的确切时间。
当你发现自己回顾这一年时，要意识到时间的错觉。
实验室和外部世界的主观持续时间。在主观时间：时间性的哲学、心理学和神经科学[pdf]
note_en:
The global coronavirus pandemic has heightened our awareness that time is subjective. For some people who enjoy working from home, the days have whizzed by. For others desperate to travel or visit a loved one, time has slowed to a crawl.
 Clocks were invented to help us track the passage of time - and yet in some moments when staring at a clock, we’re made aware of just how long a second can feel.
  Both periods of time were equal in length, though the first period - when the yellow square is visible - is commonly said to feel longer than the second period, which is the empty, or unfilled, gap between two yellow squares.
 According to neuroscientists, there is not a single organ or system in the body responsible for timekeeping. In fact, psychologists have identified many factors that affect our sense of time, some of which explain our heightened awareness of it this year.
  In this exercise, you were asked to make a time estimation after an event, or what psychologists call a retrospective judgement.
 Valtteri Arstila, a philosophy lecturer at the University of Turku in Finland, explains that you can make this judgment because you have in working memory the first shape to compare. While this method works comparing very short durations, a different process is used for longer time periods.
 Think back to when you were asked to stay home to prevent the spread of the new coronavirus. Did that period go by quickly or slowly?
 Most New Yorkers, for example, were asked to stay home beginning some time in March, and many seemed to note that April flew by despite the repetitive days.
 Craig Callender, a professor of philosophy at the University of California San Diego, explains that we’re making this judgment based on an event recalled from our longer term memory.
 “If you think of every salient event as ticks of the clock, there weren’t that many ticks in April so it feels like time went by really fast,” he said.
  You just watched a series of images that were shown for the same amount of time though the novel image, in this case the nested squares, is often thought to have lasted longer.
 This is called the oddball effect. Arstila points to two phenomena that can help us understand how we deal with periods of repetition.
 First, we already have a record of the repeated object in memory and as such we pay less attention and it feels as though it passes more quickly. Second, when the new object appears, our attention focuses on creating a new memory, causing time to feel as though it has slowed down.
 Arstila says the suppression of repetitive days is one reason that we may remember periods stuck at home as passing quickly. This same effect, he says, explains why retired people, who often have routine days, report that time flies by.
  A number of studies have looked at how attention and emotion affect our sense of time. There are challenges to recreating illustrative examples, but try the following test.
  You just performed an interval time estimation. Three stimuli were shown for the same duration, though the expanding circle is often thought to last longer.
 “Things that grab our attention or require more attention feel much longer,” said Arstila.
  “Passage of time estimates correlate best with moods people have. If you have a good time, time flies, if you are sad, lonely, then time tracks,” said Arstila.
 A busy day usually goes by quickly, but for some the opposite is true in 2020. Frontline healthcare workers, for example, know they are at high risk of exposure to the coronavirus, and the resulting anxiety heightens their attentiveness and slows their perception of how quickly a day passes.
 For others, however, time can fly during joyful moments, such as a video catch-up or dinner with friends.
  You may have found yourself recently pondering when you last met up with a friend or went to the gym. Our recall of events both recent and long ago distort in a curious pattern.
 Test yourself by thinking of a recent appointment and estimating how long ago it happened. Then check your calendar to see when it actually occurred. Or, without help, try dating the following events.
 We tend to rely on memory rather than knowledge to date events happening within our lifetime. But as our memory distorts our perception of time, it also affects our sense of when an event took place.
 Psychologists have found that it’s common when recalling a long-ago event to think that it happened more recently than it did. But if the event happened within the past three years, we often think that it happened longer ago.
 This effect is called telescoping. Think of it as looking backward or forwards through a telescope where images are distorted depending on the orientation.
 Given the shortage in toilet paper this year, can you remember the last time you bought some? Keeping in mind that memories fade over time, your recall may experience telescoping.
 2020 will not be forgotten as the year of the pandemic anytime soon, but there is a high probability that we will misplace exactly when some events occurred.
 As you find yourself looking back on this year, be aware of the illusion of time.
  Subjective duration in the laboratory and the world outside. In Subjective time: The philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience of temporality  [pdf]
title: 为什么时间在2020年感觉如此奇怪
title_en: Why time feels so weird in 2020

跳到导航跳跃以搜索惠普尔盾牌或惠普尔保险杠，由弗雷德·惠普尔发明[1]，是一种超高速撞击盾牌，用于保护载人和无人航天器免受速度通常在每秒3至18公里(1.9至11.2英里/秒)之间的微流星体和轨道碎片的碰撞。
与早期航天器的整体屏蔽不同，惠普尔屏蔽是由一个相对较薄的外部保险杠组成的，它与主航天器壁相距一定距离。保险杠预计不会阻止传入的粒子，甚至不会带走它的大部分能量，而是将其分解和分散，将原始粒子的能量分配给许多碎片，这些碎片在保险杠和墙壁之间呈扇形分布。原来的粒子能量在更大的壁面上传播得更薄，更有可能承受得住它。一个直接的比喻是，与总质量和动能相同的单个步枪子弹相比，需要更轻的防弹背心来阻止大量的鸟弹。虽然惠普尔屏蔽与实心屏蔽相比降低了航天器的总质量，但在航天飞行中，低质量总是可取的，但额外的封闭体积可能需要更大的有效载荷整流罩。
简单的惠普尔盾牌有几种变体。像“星尘”号航天器上使用的那样，多重防震护盾[2][3]使用间隔开的多个缓冲器来增加护盾保护航天器的能力。在坚硬的屏蔽层之间有填充物的惠普尔屏蔽称为填充惠普尔屏蔽。[4][5]这些屏蔽中的填料通常是高强度材料，如芳纶或Nextel氧化铝纤维。[6]屏蔽层的类型、材料、厚度和层之间的距离被改变，以产生质量最小的屏蔽层，该屏蔽层还将使穿透的可能性最小化。仅在国际空间站上就有100多种屏蔽配置[7]，高风险区域的屏蔽效果更好。

note_en:
Jump to navigation  Jump to search  The  Whipple shield or  Whipple bumper, invented by  Fred Whipple,  [1] is a type of  hypervelocity impact shield used to protect crewed and uncrewed  spacecraft from collisions with  micrometeoroids and  orbital debris whose velocities generally range between 3 and 18 kilometres per second (1.9 and 11.2 mi/s).
   In contrast to monolithic shielding of early spacecraft, Whipple shields consist of a relatively thin outer bumper spaced some distance from the main spacecraft wall. The bumper is not expected to stop the incoming particle or even remove much of its energy, but to break up and disperse it, dividing the original particle energy among many fragments that fan out between bumper and wall. The original particle energy is spread more thinly over a larger wall area, which is more likely to withstand it. A direct analogy is that a lighter  bullet resistant vest is needed to stop a load of  birdshot than a single  rifle  bullet with the same total mass and  kinetic energy. Although a Whipple shield lowers total spacecraft mass compared to a solid shield, lower mass being always desirable in spaceflight, the extra enclosed volume may require a larger  payload fairing.
 There are several variations on the simple Whipple shield.  Multi-shock shields,  [2]  [3] like the one used on the   Stardust spacecraft, use multiple bumpers spaced apart to increase the shield's ability to protect the spacecraft. Whipple shields that have a filling in between the rigid layers of the shield are called  stuffed Whipple shields.  [4]  [5] The filling in these shields is usually a high-strength material like  Kevlar or Nextel  aluminium oxide fiber.  [6] The type of shield, the material, thickness and distance between layers are varied to produce a shield with minimal mass that will also minimize the probability of penetration. There are over 100 shield configurations on the  International Space Station alone,  [7] with higher-risk areas having better shielding.

title: 惠普尔盾牌

note:
加里·拉森(Gary Larson)刚刚为“遥远的彼岸”发行了新漫画，这是自1995年1月以来的第一幅漫画。虽然拉森告诫说，这“不是远方每日漫画的复活”，在重读了25年我们最喜欢的漫画之后，我们对新作品感到如此兴奋，以至于我们愿意接受他想分享的任何东西。去年年底，拉森开始将他的漫画放到网上。
新漫画的第一部以熊、外星人和标本为特色(都是另一边的主食)。这种风格很熟悉，但有两个大的例外：新漫画不是用水彩，而是用数字笔触制作，让图像感觉比原来的全色卡通更有体积和活力。此外，水彩画和黑白原创漫画中都存在的书面轮廓几乎完全消失了。最终的结果是图像唤起了老漫画的感觉，但不知何故变得不那么卡通了。所有的人物和元素都感觉在场景中统一在一起。
“我被这个东西提供的所有工具和它所蕴含的所有创造潜力惊呆了。”
风格的改变和漫画的回归都是因为拉森现在使用的是数字平板电脑。在多年与堵塞的钢笔和干涸的记号笔打交道后，拉森决定给数字化一个机会。“我被这个东西提供的所有工具和它所蕴含的所有创造潜力惊呆了。”我根本不知道这些东西已经演变到什么程度了，“拉森在他新作品的标题”新材料“(New Stuff)的开场信中写道。“也许恰如其分的是，我画的第一幅画是一个山顶洞人。”
拉森并不是第一个用钢笔换手写笔的漫画家。诱惑力相当强。使用平板电脑打开了各种各样的数字画笔和工具，以及在对象和着色的层中工作的能力-这些选项在拉森的系列第一次运行时是不存在的。为了模仿钢笔和屏幕保护器，让它感觉像是在纸上绘图，存在的一系列触控笔也允许更流畅的过渡。
在信的末尾，拉森要求读者记住，“我只是在探索、试验和尝试一些东西。”这是令人兴奋的。有了过多的新工具可供他使用，我们急切地想看看它们将如何用来重振遥远的世界，尽管拉森很少抽出时间来做这件事。
note_en:
Gary Larson just released  new comics for  The Far Side, the first strips since January 1995. While Larson cautions that this is “not a resurrection of  The Far Side daily cartoons,” after 25 years of rereading our favorites, we’re so excited for new work that we’ll take anything he wants to share. Larson started to  bring his comics online late last year.
 The first of the new comics features bears, aliens, and taxidermy (all staples of  The Far Side). The style is comfortably familiar, with two large exceptions: instead of watercolor, the new comics are done in digital brushstrokes that make the images feel more volumetric and vibrant than the  original full-color cartoons. Also, the penned outlines, which exist in both the watercolor and black-and-white original comics, are almost entirely gone. The end result is images that evoke the feel of the old comics but are somehow a little less  cartoony. The characters and elements all feel unified in the scene together.
 “I was stunned at all the tools the thing offered, all the creative potential it contained.”
 Both the style changes and the comic’s return are due to the fact that Larson is now using a digital tablet. After years of frustration dealing with clogged pens and dried-up markers, Larson decided to give going digital a chance. “I was stunned at all the tools the thing offered, all the creative potential it contained. I simply had no idea how far these things had evolved,” Larson writes in an opening letter for  New Stuff, the title for his new works. “Perhaps fittingly, the first thing I drew was a caveman.”
 Larson isn’t the first cartoonist to trade in a pen for a stylus. The allure is pretty strong. Using a tablet opens up a wide variety of digital brushes and tools as well as the ability to work in layers for objects and coloring — options that didn’t exist when Larson’s series first ran. The range of styluses that exist to mimic pens and screen protectors that make it feel like drawing on paper allow for a smoother transition, too.
 At the end of the letter, Larson asks readers to remember, “I’m just exploring, experimenting, and trying stuff.” And that is exciting. With a plethora of new tools at his disposal, we’re eager to see how they’ll be used to revive  The Far Side, however infrequently Larson gets around to it.
title: 25年后，另一边又回来了，而且都是数字化的
title_en: The Far Side Returns After 25 Years, and It's All Digital
